ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boiled okra as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for individual boiled vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g (one plate) of boiled okra, equivalent to roughly 8–10 whole okra pods.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Okra, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 170383)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for okra cooked by boiling without salt is an exact match for this preparation. Values are cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for okra. Sodium value from input (166.67 mg/100g) is notably higher than USDA boiled-without-salt value (~4 mg/100g) and likely reflects a salted-water boiling preparation or inclusion of a dipping sauce component; the USDA value for plain boiled okra is used as the primary nutrient anchor. The input sodium figure is retained as an adapted estimate reflecting typical Vietnamese preparation with lightly salted boiling water, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled okra exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Okra is a non-starchy vegetable with very low available carbohydrate content and high soluble fibre (mucilaginous polysaccharides) that significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. A GI of approximately 20 is estimated by analogy with other low-GI non-starchy boiled vegetables (e.g., green beans GI ~15, courgette GI ~15) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 Atkinson et al. international GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (20 × 7.45 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.24, rounded to 2.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium when boiled without added salt; high dietary fibre and potassium support blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Plain boiled okra contains only ~4 mg sodium per 100 g and provides approximately 135 mg potassium per 100 g, making it an excellent choice for a DASH-aligned diet; patients should avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as fish sauce or soy sauce to preserve the sodium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~20), high soluble dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g) including mucilaginous polysaccharides that slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled okra has an estimated GI of approximately 20 and a glycemic load of only 2.2 per 150 g serving, making it highly suitable for blood glucose management; the soluble fibre content also supports insulin sensitivity, and regular inclusion in meals is clinically encouraged for individuals with type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high soluble fibre that supports LDL cholesterol reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled okra is a cardioprotective vegetable with negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, and its soluble fibre content (3.2 g/100 g) contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction; it is suitable as a regular component of a heart-healthy dietary pattern, provided it is not served with high-fat or high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in vitamin C (16.3 mg/100 g) and antioxidants; low-fat anti-inflammatory vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled okra provides vitamin C and dietary antioxidants that may support reduction of oxidative stress in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should note that okra is not a nightshade, though any chilli-based dipping sauce should be used cautiously.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled okra (Đậu Bắp Luộc) is a simple Vietnamese side dish consisting of whole okra pods (lady's fingers) that are boiled in lightly salted water until just tender, typically 5–8 minutes, and served warm or at room temperature alongside a dipping sauce such as fish sauce with chilli and garlic (nước chấm) or a simple soy-based sauce. The okra retains a slightly mucilaginous, tender texture with a mild, grassy flavour. It is a common home-cooked and restaurant side dish in Vietnam and among Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, boiled okra is a low-calorie, low-fat vegetable providing approximately 33 kcal per 100 g, with 1.86 g protein, 0.19 g fat, and 7.45 g carbohydrate. It is an excellent source of dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g), including soluble mucilaginous polysaccharides that slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial blood glucose rise, giving it an estimated glycemic index of approximately 20. It also provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (16.3 mg/100 g), folate (46 mcg DFE/100 g), calcium (77 mg/100 g), and potassium (135 mg/100 g). Clinically, boiled okra is highly suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its low GI, low sodium, negligible saturated fat, and cholesterol-lowering soluble fibre. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. The dish is typically served as a side vegetable alongside rice and a protein dish in a Vietnamese meal.
common_user_queries: Is boiled okra good for diabetes?
Boiled okra for high blood pressure
Đậu bắp luộc bao nhiêu calo

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170383), which is an exact match for boiled okra without added salt — an approved primary source. GI is estimated by analogy with closely comparable non-starchy boiled vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database, with the estimation basis clearly documented. Vietnamese local name is confirmed. All mandatory sections are complete.
notes: 1. Sodium value: The input record reported 166.67 mg sodium/100 g (from GrabFood/AI-generated source), which likely reflects boiling in lightly salted water or inclusion of a dipping sauce component. The USDA FoodData Central value for boiled okra without added salt is 4 mg/100 g, which is used as the primary nutrient anchor for plain boiled okra. Users should be advised that sodium content will increase significantly if a fish sauce or soy sauce dipping sauce is consumed. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for boiled okra; naturally zero in this plant food. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for this preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are available from USDA FoodData Central and are populated. 4. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for boiled okra exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as the closest defensible match for boiled okra; this is a broad vegetable category code and should be refined if a more specific FoodEx2 code becomes available. 6. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish is a Vietnamese preparation, though it is commonly consumed in Singapore.
